coredata.dc:title: Microwave synthesis of amphiphilic carbon dots from xylose and construction of luminescent composites with shape recovery performance
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Ziqi
Yang, Pei
Chen, Minzhi
Zhang, Tao
Cao, Yizhong
Zhang, Wei
Zhou, Xiaoyan
Chen, Weimin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have sparked tremendous attention owing to their unique properties and great potential in various fields. Herein, novel amphiphilic CDs (ACDs) were synthesized solvothermally by microwave heating a mixture of xylose and ethylenediamine in N, N-dimethylacetamide. The xylose-derived ACDs were monodisperse nanoparticles with an average diameter of about 1.81 nm, and exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior in both aqueous and several common organic solvents. Furthermore, the synthesized ACDs can be uniformly dispersed into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution to fabricate transparent luminescent composites, the as-prepared PVA/ACDs composites not only exhibited excellent fluorescence with excitation-dependent emission performance, but also achieved water-induced shape memory behavior. The intrinsic reason and pH sensitivity of shape recovery were investigated, the results confirmed that a faster shape recovery can be obtained in water at pH 4 within 180 s. Further investigation illustrated that the sensitivity of additional hydrogen bonding between PVA and ACDs to the water molecular was highly related to the shape recovery of the luminescent composite. The green synthesis route for xylan-derived ACDs and fabrication of smart luminescent composite will open many possibilities for the valuable application of low-cost biomass.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Microwave
Amphiphilic
Luminescent film
Shape memory

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Shape memory performance of PVA/ACDs composite film
Characterization and optical properties of PVA/ACDs films

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic route of ACDs using xylose and EDA precursors. (b) TEM image of ACDs. (c) HRTEM image of ACDs. (d) The particle size distribution. (e) FT-IR spectra of xylose and ACDs. (f) XPS survey spectrum, (g) C1s spectra, (h) N1s spectra, and (i) O1s spectra of ACDs.

(a) UV–Vis absorption spectrum and PL excitation spectrum of ACDs. (b) PL emission spectra of ACDs in alcohol solution at different excitation wavelengths. Inset is the photograph of ACDs in alcohol solution under ambient light and UV light. (c) PL decay curve of ACDs irradiated at 410 nm and the fitting equation is shown in the corresponding panel. PL emission spectra of ACDs in (d) purified water, (e) trichloromethane and (f) methylbenzene. Insets: photographs of solutions taken under ambient and UV light.
Photographs of PVA films embedded with ACDs under ambient light (up) and UV light (down). ACDs mass concentration in the PVA films (left to right): 0, 0.04, 0.09, 0.14, 0.17, and 0.21%.
(a–e) PL emission spectra of the PVA/ACDs films with different excitation wavelengths. (f) PL intensity variation of PVA/ACDs films at different excitation wavelengths.
Shape recovery of (a) PVA/ACDs-0.09% composite films in water at pH = 4, pH = 6 and pH = 8 under 365 nm UV lamp (b) the pure PVA film in water at pH = 4 under the daylight at room temperature.
(a) The FT-IR spectra of PVA and PVA/ACDs −0.09% composites. (b) The TGA curves of pure PVA and PVA/ACDs-0.09% films.

head.ce:title: Microwave synthesis of amphiphilic carbon dots from xylose and construction of luminescent composites with shape recovery performance

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing, 210037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have sparked tremendous attention owing to their unique properties and great potential in various fields. Herein, novel amphiphilic CDs (ACDs) were synthesized solvothermally by microwave heating a mixture of xylose and ethylenediamine in N, N-dimethylacetamide. The xylose-derived ACDs were monodisperse nanoparticles with an average diameter of about 1.81 nm, and exhibited excitation-dependent emission behavior in both aqueous and several common organic solvents. Furthermore, the synthesized ACDs can be uniformly dispersed into polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) solution to fabricate transparent luminescent composites, the as-prepared PVA/ACDs composites not only exhibited excellent fluorescence with excitation-dependent emission performance, but also achieved water-induced shape memory behavior. The intrinsic reason and pH sensitivity of shape recovery were investigated, the results confirmed that a faster shape recovery can be obtained in water at pH 4 within 180 s. Further investigation illustrated that the sensitivity of additional hydrogen bonding between PVA and ACDs to the water molecular was highly related to the shape recovery of the luminescent composite. The green synthesis route for xylan-derived ACDs and fabrication of smart luminescent composite will open many possibilities for the valuable application of low-cost biomass.

ce:section.ce:para: During the past few years, the fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention. In the light of abundant precursors, high photostability, easy functionalization, low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility [1–5], CDs are considered as promising candidates for the quantum dots (QDs) and exhibit great potential application in various fields such as bioimaging [6,7], light-emitting diodes [8,9], catalysis [10], chemical sensing [11–13], ions testing [14–16] and so on. To date, it is still of a continuous and considerable interest to develop CDs in an efficient, facile and green synthesis route. Myriad CDs have been prepared based on top-down and bottom-up approaches, such as arc-discharge method [4], laser-ablation method [17,18], ultrasonic processing [19] and hydrothermal method [15,20–22]. Among these methods, the bottom-up strategy has been confirmed to be superior in selecting precursors, optimizing synthesis conditions and adjusting the solvents. However, the conventional bottom-up approaches always require toxic chemical reagents, rigorous synthesis conditions, and long reaction time. Considering that the microwave irradiation can provide intensive and homogeneous energy distribution in the reaction system of precursor, it is more effective than the traditional hydrothermal or solvothermal treatment in accelerating the reaction rate in the liquid phase. Hence, a microwave synthesis system, which was produced by the Chemistry Electronic Microwave Company (DISCOVER SP, America), was used to synthesize CDs in the present investigation.

Thus far, despite massive efforts have been devoted to synthesis CDs from various carbon sources, the expensive or even poisonous starting materials are the main limiting factor for the sustainable development of CDs. Therefore, the CDs synthesized from cost-efficient and avirulent carbon sources is still an issue to be addressed. Recent years have witnessed the carbon-rich biomass is an extremely attractive carbon source for the preparation of carbon microspheres [23], activated carbon [24], aerogel carbon [25] and so on. Similarly, the biomass has been confirmed to be a promising carbon source for the fabrication of CDs. Herein, the xylose was chosen as the carbon source by its virtues of low-cost, abundant carbon content and low carbonization temperature. In addition, the increasing researches have demonstrated that doping of N element within the structures of CDs is an effective way to improve the photoluminescence efficiency of CDs by changing the electron density of surface states [3]. Thus, ethylenediamine (EDA) was employed as N dopant because of its high nitrogen content, low-cost and effectiveness during the formation of functionalized CDs.

Over the past decade, considerable progress has been achieved in the preparation and application of hydrophilic CDs, while the synthesis of amphiphilic CDs (ACDs) is still in their infancy stage. The ACDs with hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties impart their wide application in various fields, such as bio-imaging for liposoluble cell and the fabrication of composite materials with commodity polymers [26,27]. Therefore, the ACDs are more likely to open up a new avenue in different environments. Jelinek et al. prepared ACDs with long hydrocarbon chains and the resultant nanostructures were readily bind to bacterial cells [28]. Gu et al. reported a low-cost solvothermal route for the synthesis of ACDs from a range of readily acquirable organics solvents, and the obtained ACDs demonstrated their potential application in fluorescent cellular imaging [29]. Tan et al. synthesized ACDs by using oil soluble N-doped CDs successfully [30]. However, these current synthesis routes for ACDs are usually unsatisfactory in the addition of poisonous reagents in precursor. Hence, the cost-effective and green synthesis route is highly desirable for the development of ACDs. In this present work, novel ACDs with excellent fluorescence are synthesized from a precursor comprising of xylose and EAD in N, N-dimethylacetamide (DMAc) via an ultrafast (45 min) microwave-assisted solvothermal method. Moreover, to realize efficient solid-state light-emitting composite, a series of CDs/polymer films have been fabricated through integrating the ACDs with polyvinyl alcohol (PVA), the obtained luminescent films are demonstrated to be promising candidates for application in photoelectric devices.

Xylose (98%), quinine sulfate (99.0%) and PVA (MW ≈ 145,000) were purchased from Aladdin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Purified water (Hangzhou, China) was used in all experiments. EDA was purchased from Shanghai Hansi Chemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). DMAc, ethyl alcohol, chloroform and methylbenzene were procured from Nanjing Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Nanjing, China). All chemicals were used as received without further purification.

Fluorescent ACDs were prepared by microwave irradiation of DMAc solution in the presence of xylose and EDA. In a typical synthesis, 0.5 g xylose was dissolved in 10 mL of DMAc solution, followed by the introduction of 500 μL EDA as the passivant. Subsequently, the mixture was directly placed in a microwave synthesizer and irradiated at 200 °C and 200 W for 45 min. After the reaction cooled down to room temperature, the resultant dark brown solution was filtered by a 0.22 μm syringe filter to remove large particles. Then the filtrates were poured into a round-bottomed flask and concentrated by a rotary evaporation to obtain the solid ACDs. Finally, the black dried sample was collected, measured and dispersed in ethanol. The obtained solid ACDs sample and the diluted ACDs solution with a concentration of 170.8 mg/mL were used for further characterization.

To fully dissolve the powder polymer, 2 g PVA powder was added in 30 mL purified water and heated at 90 °C. Subsequently, a series of mixtures were prepared by adding varying amount of ACDs into the PVA solutions, which made the mass ration of ACDs to PVA were 0.04, 0.09, 0.14, 0.17, and 0.21%. These obtained mixtures with yellow in color were poured into a 150 mm glass petri dish, and additional vacuum processing was used to remove the bubbles in the solution to ensure the obtained films smooth. Then, the coated dishes were placed in a dryer at 60 °C till the purified water completely evaporated. Finally, a series of composite films were stripped off from the dish and stored for further experiments. For convenience, the obtained composites containing different mass ratio of ACDs/PVA were denoted as PVA/ACDs-0.04%, −0.09%, −0.14%, −0.17%, and −0.21%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Shape memory performance of PVA/ACDs composite film
ce:section.ce:para: A bending test was measured to probe the shape memory performance of the composite films. The pure PVA films and PVA/ACDs composite films were first cut into straight strips (40 mm × 10 mm × 0.2 mm) and bent to an angle close to 180°. Then the rectangles were placed in a dry oven at 90 °C for 10 min and the bent strip was further cooled to room temperature under external force to obtain the deformed shape. Subsequently, the shape recovery performance was investigated by immersing the deformed strip into water with different pH values to record the changes of bending shapes with the increase of immersion time.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained using a JEM-2100 UHR (JEOL, Japan) at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Raman spectra were recorded on a Lab DXR 532 spectrometer (Thermo, American) under the pumping if a 532 nm pulsed laser. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded on VERTEX 80 V spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) ranging from 500 to 4000 cm−1 with the KBr pellets technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on the AXIS UltraDLD spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using a NETZSCH 209F3 (Germany) at a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 from 30 to 200 °C under a nitrogen flow. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption of ACDs solution was carried out on Lambda 950 (PE, American). Photoluminescence (PL) was measured with an F-7000 spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). The surface charge of ACDs was performed on a ZEN3600 Zeta Plus (Malvern, United Kingdom) instrument with a He–Ne laser source. Time-resolved fluorescence decays were determined using a FLS 980 spectrometer (EI, UK) under excitation at 410 nm and the obtained lifetime decay curves were fitted using a double-exponential function y(t) (eq. (1)) and the average lifetime was calculated according to eq. (2).
(1)

)
here, A1 and A2 are the fractional contributions of time-resolved fluorescence decay lifetimes τ1 and τ2, respectively, and τ* refers to the average lifetime.

The quantum yield of the as-prepared ACDs was measured by a well-established comparative method using quinine sulfate as standard. Typically, quinine sulfate with QY = 0.54 was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 (refractive index of 1.33) while the ACDs were dissolved into ethyl alcohol (refractive index of 1.36), their absorption values and fluorescent spectra were measured and the QY of ACDs was calculated by the following equation:
(3)
Q
x
=
Q
s t
(
I
x
/
I
s t
)
(
A
s t
/
A
x
)
(
η
x
2
/
η
s t
2
)
here, Q stands for quantum yield. Grad refers to the gradient of the plot of integrated fluorescence peak area against absorbance. η represents the refractive index of the solvent. The subscripts x and st correspond to the ACDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. In particular, the absorbance was adjusted to less than 0.05 to prevent re-absorption by both quinine sulfate and ACDs solutions.

The ACDs synthesized from xylose following the procedure depicted in Fig. 1 a, and the structure and size of ACDs were characterized in detail by TEM. As shown in Fig. 1b, the TEM image reveals a large quantity of quasi-spherical nanostructures without noticeable aggregation at room temperature. Moreover, the high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image of ACDs exhibits the lattice fringes with a interplanar spacing of 0.21 nm, which is close to the (100) lattice space of graphitic carbon (Fig. 1c) [31,32]. Briefly, the main reason for the crystalline nature of xylose-derived ACDs can be put down to intensive microwave irradiation and high steam pressure in the synthesis reaction. The particle size distribution of ACDs was determined as shown in Fig. 1d, it can be observed from the histogram that the ACDs have a narrow size distribution of 0.5–3.0 nm with an average diameter of 1.81 nm by judging from 100 individual particles. These monodisperse nanoparticles with small size are more likely to minimize the steric hindrance effect effectively, which is beneficial to the copolymerization during the fabrication of fluorescent polymer materials [33].

In an effort to explore the chemical compositions and functional groups in the ACDs, FT-IR and XPS analyses were conducted. As shown in the FT-IR spectrum of xylose (Fig. 1e), the broad absorption band centered at 3214 cm−1 is attributed to O–H stretching vibration. A series of characteristic bands around 1030 cm−1, 1128 cm−1 and 2896 cm−1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of C–O–C, C–O and C–H, respectively. The intense bands at 910 cm−1, 1342 cm−1 and 1456 cm−1 can be assigned to the bending vibration of C–H, C–OH and –CH2. In terms of the FT-IR spectrum of ACDs, the absorption bands display several characteristics of xylose, such as the stretching vibration of –OH at 3413 cm−1 and the stretching vibration of C–O–C at 1030 cm−1. Notably, a broad band around 3278 cm−1 overlapped with –OH stretching vibration reveals the presence of amino groups on the surface of ACDs, whereas the peaks at 2931 and 2870 cm−1 are related to the stretching vibration of –CH2 and –CH3, In addition, the sharp peak at 1629 cm−1 is ascribed to CC stretching vibration and the emerging peak at 1548 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of N–H. Although at low intensity, the absorption peaks at 1434 and 1375 cm−1 are related to the C–N stretching vibration. Moreover, the absorption band at 1118 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of C–O. Such differences in FT-IR spectrum between xylose and ACDs demonstrate that there is a remarkable chemical transformation when the xylose is subjected to intensive microwave treatment. The FT-IR assignments of ACDs are further corroborated by XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectrum of ACDs (Fig. 1f) displays three dominant peaks located at about 284.9, 406.9 and 532.5 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s and O1s, respectively. These findings indicate that the as-synthesized ACDs mainly consist of C (77.1%), N (13.4%), and O (9.5%) elements, in which the high content of N element in ACDs is more likely to originate from N doping effect of EDA. To gain the elemental speciation, the signal of C1s N1s and O1s were analyzed in detail. The high resolution of C1s spectrum of ACDs (Fig. 1g) is well-fitted into C–C/CC (284.4 eV), C–N (285.3 eV), C–O (286.3 eV), and CO (287.6 eV) [34]. Specifically, the C–O component associate with the intramolecular dehydration in the formation of ACDs, while the C–C/CC, which is the skeleton of ACDs, is resulted from intramolecular dehydration. The N1s band (Fig. 1h) can be deconvoluted into two peaks with the binding energy of 399.2 eV and 400.2 eV, corresponding to pyrrolic-N and –NH2 [35]. In addition, the deconvolution of O1s spectra reveals the presence of CO, and C–OH or C–O–C within the structure of ACDs (Fig. 1i). Zeta potential of the synthesized ACDs was also measured to illustrate the charge characteristics of their surface, and the result suggests that the synthesized ACDs are positively charged (0.59 mV), thus further demonstrating the attachment of nitrogen-containing groups on the surface of ACDs. From the above analysis, we have identified that the synthesized ACDs are composed of carbon cores with aromatic structures and multiple functional groups. It is reasonable to deduced that the ordered packing of alkyl chains and several hydrophobic functional groups are responsible for the hydrophobic property of ACDs, while the existence of hydrophilic groups, such as amino hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, result in the excellent stability and dispersibility of ACDs in water. Thus, the fabricated ACDs could be possessed of great amphiphilic performance without any further chemical modification.

The optical properties of ACDs in alcohol solution were investigated by UV–Vis absorption and PL spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the UV–vis spectrum of resultant ACDs exhibits a distinct absorption peak centered at 330 nm with a tail extending into the visible region, this characteristic absorption peak should be attributed to the n-π* transition of the CO band. Notably, it can be observed that the peak of excitation spectrum overlaps with the absorption band, such performance indicates that the strong PL emission of ACDs is mainly related to the trapping of excited state energy of the surface functional groups [36]. Additionally, to further probe the optical properties of the as-prepared ACDs, the PL emission behavior was investigated and depicted in Fig. 2b. It is obvious that the PL intensities of ACDs in alcohol solution decreased gradually, accompanying with the red-shift of the emission peaks when the excitation wavelength increased from 360 nm to 460 nm. Moreover, the maximum PL emission of ACDs is confirmed at 439 nm when the ACDs are excited at 360 nm, which is consistent with the result of excitation spectrum. Commonly, the excitation-dependent PL emission performance can be ascribed to different defect states on CDs surface, it is worth mention that these defects within the structures of ACDs should be heterogeneous, thus resulting different emission-energy levels for the ACDs [37,38]. The as-prepared ACDs dispersed in alcoholic solution is faint-yellow under ambient light, upon illumination with a 365 nm UV lamp, the ACDs showed bright blue luminescence (insert of Fig. 2b). The time-resolved fluorescence decay trace was measured to gain insight in to the PL properties of ACDs, as shown in Fig. 2c. The decay curve can be fitted into a double-exponential functional with short lifetimes (τ1) of 1.21 ns and long lifetimes (τ2) of 5.05 ns, and an average lifetime of 5.05 ns was also determined based on the fitting of the PL decay curve. The determined average lifetime on ns level suggests the possible PL mechanism is the radioactive recombination of photogenerated electrons. Both of the double-exponential decay and excitation-dependent emission properties imply the multiple emission centers within the structures of ACDs.

To enforce the evidence of amphiphilic performance of fabricated ACDs, the PL emission of ACDs in purified water, trichloromethane and methylbenzene were investigated, respectively. As shown in Fig. 2(d–f), the emission spectra obtained from the ACDs dissolved in the mentioned different solutions display typical excitation-dependent emission behaviors. Besides, these four prepared ACDs solutions have been confirmed to emit bright and uniform blue luminescence without obvious agglomeration after three months of storage (insets of Fig. 2(d–f)), suggesting that the synthesized ACDs possess excellent hydrophilic and hydrophobic properties. It is worth mention that the optimal excitation wavelength of ACDs in ethyl alcohol, trichloromethane and methylbenzene centered at 360 nm, while the aqueous ACDs solution exhibits blue-shift of optimal excitation. Previous reports have pointed out that the effect of solvent on optimal excitation of CDs could be related to the intramolecular charge transfer [39]. Furthermore, the fluorescence quantum yield of ACDs in alcohol, water, trichloromethane and methylbenzene were determined as 9.0%, 8.1%, 5.4% and 4.8% at their corresponding optimal excitation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of PVA/ACDs films
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized ACDs can be well dissolved into PVA solution and a series of luminescent composite films with almost the same thickness were fabricated in our case. As shown in Fig. 3 , the PVA films contained ACDs were yellow color and the color became slightly darker with the increasing content of ACDs. Obviously, the resultant composite films displayed excellent transmittance even the PVA/ACDs mixture contain 0.21%wt of ACDs. Furthermore, the PVA film without the immobilization of ACDs has no PL emission, while all the as-prepared PVA/ACDs films contained ACDs emit blue light under UV-light irradiation. Such phenomenon demonstrates that the ACDs are the only fluorescence source to solid emission films.

The fluorescence of as-prepared composite films immobilized ACDs were studied by investigating their emission spectra, as shown in Fig. 4 (a–e). The emission peaks of all PVA/ACDs films locate at 410 nm with the excitation wavelengths at about 340 nm. Similar to the ACDs solution, these films contained ACDs also exhibit an excitation-dependent PL emission behavior, while the maximum emission peak of luminescent films shifts to the short wavelength compare to that of ACDs in ethanol solution. The possible reason is that the hydrogen bonding between the ACDs and PVA has large effect on emission trap sites of ACDs through the altering of surface electronic structures [39,40]. Additionally, Fig. 4f illustrates that the resulting luminescent films display the largest PL intensity at 340 nm excitation when the weight ratio of ACDs and PVA is 0.09. This phenomenon illustrates that higher ACDs content can result in a decrease of PL intensity due to a variety of interrelated factors such as inner filter effect, PL reabsorption, and partial quenching on account of the particle agglomeration [41]. Because of their stable and excellent fluorescent properties, the fabricated PVA/ACDs composite film is promising color conversion medium for LED illumination.

The as-prepared ACDs have been confirmed to maintain excellent PL performance when they were encapsulated in PVA films. For further exploring ACDs-based composites and enlarge application scope of xylose-derived CDs, we found that the fabricated luminescent films exhibit excellent water-responsive shape memory behavior. For detailed investigation of the shape recovery property, the luminescent film that fabricated from ACDs and PVA with a mass ratio of 0.09 was used as an example. As shown in Fig. 5 (a), it is clearly observed that different conditions result in different recovery velocities of films. The sample can entirely recover to the original shape after being immersed in distilled water at pH 4 for about 180 s, while the shape recovery velocity is decreased with the increasing of pH. The control experiments using pure PVA film were also carried out, it can be clearly seen that the pure PVA can hardly recover in distilled water at pH 4 (Fig. 5 (b)). Thus, we conclude that the synthesized ACDs have capacity to endow the PVA polymer with excellent shape recovery performance, such performance can be ascribed to the enhanced physically cross linking by adding ACDs into PVA [42]. When water molecules penetrate into the deformed composite, the original hydrogen bonding in the composite would be destroyed by competitive hydrogen bonding from water [43]. Furthermore, it can be deduced that the crystallinity of PVA was decreased and the plasticizing effect is resisted in water at pH 4, which results in the increasing of shape recovery velocity of PVA/ACDs composites.

To better confirm the interactions between PVA matrix and ACDs, the FT-IR spectroscopy is conducted to illustrate the differences between the pure PVA film and PVA/ACDs composite film, as shown in Fig. 6 a. For the PVA/ACDs composites, the peak position of –OH stretching vibration shift to lower wavenumber (from 3288 cm−1 to 3268 cm−1), such evolution has been expounded in previous works, and the main reason associate with association/disassociation of hydrogen bonding [42,44]. This change should be attributed to the disassociation of the intramolecular hydrogen bonds of PVA and the presence of additional hydrogen bonding interactions between PVA and ACDs. A large number of physical cross-linking points help to reduce chain slippage and thus strengthen the hard domains [45]. Additionally, the interactions between PVA and ACDs can also be demonstrated by TGA results (Fig. 6b). The TGA curves show that Tg of PVA/ACDs composite film is higher than that of pure PVA film, which may be assigned to the additional hydrogen bonding via interfacial interaction between PVA and ACDs [46].

In summary, we developed a simple one-step microwave-assisted pyrolysis synthesis method to prepare ACDs by using low-cost xylose in DMAc. The as-prepared fluorescent nanostructures displayed dominant excitation-dependent emission behavior in aqueous and several common organic media, such excellent amphiphilic performance was attributed the attachment of alkyl chains, amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surfaces of ACDs. By mixing the ACDs into PVA solution, luminescent composite films were obtained with excitation-dependent emission behavior. Since the additional hydrogen bonding that introduced by ACDs in the composite can be largely affected by water molecular and environmental pH value, the fabricated PVA/ACDs composite film exhibited water-induced shape memory performance at room temperature, and achieved a faster recovery velocity in water at pH 4. The favorable shape memory feature will make the ACDs-based composite potentially useful in diverse fields such as smart fabric, biomimetic materials, and stimuli-responsive drug release.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 31870549), the Jiangsu Nature Science Foundation (BK20161524), the Program for 333 Talents Project in Jiangsu Province (Grant No. BRA2016381), the Postgraduate Research &Practice Innovation Program of Jiangsu Province (KYCX17_0839), the Doctorate Fellowship Foundation of Nanjing Forestry University (2017) and the Advanced Analysis and Testing Center of Nanjing Forestry University.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2019.05.006.
Declarations of interest: none.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Luminescent oil-soluble carbon dots toward white light emission: a spectroscopic study
J. Phys. Chem. C
